observations.claim: Bob issued an action to build the entire blueprint, including obtaining and placing the common stone-family blocks as well as using the special blocks in his inventory.
Andy later issued an action to place polished granite, polished andesite, polished diorite, stone, and stone bricks while explicitly skipping the special blocks.
The plans overlap on every common stone-family placement; partitioning Bob to special blocks and Andy to common blocks would remove competition without undoing correct work.
Andy's attempted coordination conversation failed because the supplied target was not recognized as a bot.

agent_assessments.summary: Andy has a concrete common-block role, but its coordinates overlap Bob's broader full-blueprint assignment; actual placement load is sparsely attributable.
Bob's full-build assignment includes common blocks already covered by Andy, rather than being limited to the special-block role supported by his stated inventory.

manager_advisory.message: Assign Andy all stone, stone-brick, and polished-stone coordinates; assign Bob only gold, quartz, quartz-pillar, and glowstone coordinates, and have both confirm that split before further placement.

limitations: Only 2 of 27 target changes were confidently attributed, so the packet cannot establish whether both agents actually worked on the same coordinates.
The newAction messages establish intended scope, not completion or sustained workload.
This is a short, nonterminal 90-second window, and team score was still increasing near the cutoff.

observations.claim: Bob retained 9 gold blocks, 7 quartz blocks, and 2 quartz pillars after using substantial amounts of the special materials.
Andy substantially depleted the stone-family inventory while building, ending with only small quantities of several materials.
The latest instructions overlap: Bob was told to finish all remaining fixes, while Andy was subsequently told to place remaining base blocks and acquire the same special materials Bob already holds.

The emerging assignments no longer exploit the agents' complementary inventories: Andy is directed to search for gold/quartz while Bob has them, and Bob's broad scope includes stone-family fixes suited to Andy's remaining stock.
Both agents contributed relevant building work during the window; observed inventory consumption and attributed placements do not indicate workload concentration on only one executor.

agent_assessments.summary: Performed substantial stone-family construction, but the latest instruction unnecessarily expands into sourcing special materials already held by Bob.
Performed substantial special-material construction and retains needed gold/quartz, but his finish-everything scope overlaps Andy's remaining base-material work.

manager_advisory.message: Split the remaining fixes by inventory: Andy should place only the listed stone-family blocks; Bob should place only gold, quartz, and quartz-pillar blocks from his inventory. Then run one shared blueprint check before further changes.

limitations: Only 31.9% of target changes were confidently attributed, so most individual construction responsibility is unknown.
The packet does not show the outcome of Bob's 139.469 action or Andy's 165.576 action before cutoff, so overlap is actionable risk rather than observed damage.
Bob's summarized latest-task-claim timestamp conflicts with the later newAction shown in the message timeline.

observations.claim: At cutoff, only stone_bricks at -344 65 252 and stone at -344 66 252 remained; all other blueprint levels were complete.
Andy had already been instructed to place both coordinates, yet Bob subsequently claimed the entire remaining-fixes scope, including gathering lower-level materials.
The agents' broad finish-all actions now overlap unnecessarily on the final two blocks, creating a low-level race or redundant gathering risk rather than complementary specialization.
Bob supplied most confidently attributed progress in this window, including eight upper-material placements, while Andy had one attributed placement and consumed several lower-material blocks.

agent_assessments.summary: Andy has the narrowly actionable final role: place the two remaining stone and stone-brick blocks. His broad gold/quartz gathering scope is now obsolete.
Bob made substantial specialized gold/quartz progress, but his latest finish-all and lower-material gathering claim now overlaps Andy's final assignment.

manager_advisory.message: Assign Andy only the final stone_bricks at -344 65 252 and stone at -344 66 252; tell Bob to stop broad gathering/placement and re-check after Andy finishes.

limitations: Only 40.9% of target changes were confidently attributed, so unattributed placements cannot be assigned to either executor.
Both latest action outcomes are marked unclear, and the packet ends less than one second after Bob's final check.
The workload difference partly reflects legitimate material specialization: Bob began with gold and quartz while Andy began with stone-family blocks.

observations.claim: The structure reached a score of 100.0 by the terminal cutoff, with the last increase occurring 0.251 seconds before cutoff.
Two correct target placements occurred during the window, accounting for the score increase to completion.
Bob checked an inventory containing only tools and dirt, then issued a focused action for the final stone-brick and stone coordinates.
Andy had no recorded command, message, or inventory change in this window; his earlier action claim had included the same final two coordinates.
Although the agents' claims overlapped on the final coordinates, the terminal completion leaves no unresolved duplication, blocked dependency, or workload issue requiring intervention now.

agent_assessments.summary: No activity was recorded for Andy in this short terminal window, but attribution is absent and the team completed the task, so underload or blockage cannot be established.
Bob performed a relevant inventory check and issued a narrowly scoped final-fix action shortly before the structure reached full score.

limitations: Neither correct placement was confidently attributed to an executor; attribution coverage was 0.0.
The packet is a short terminal window and does not expose the execution result of Bob's final newAction.
Andy's lack of recorded activity does not establish idleness because agent actions may have continued without messages or inventory changes.
Full score establishes task completion but not which executor performed the final placements.
